mixed.question: Which planet in our solar system has the most moons?
mixed.fact: Saturn has over 140 confirmed moons, surpassing Jupiter's count in recent discoveries!

mixed.question: What is the hardest natural substance on Earth?
mixed.fact: Diamonds are so hard they can only be scratched by other diamonds!

mixed.question: In what year did the Berlin Wall fall?
mixed.fact: The wall fell on November 9, 1989, partly due to a confused press conference announcement!

mixed.question: Which country consumes the most coffee per capita?
mixed.fact: Finns drink about 12 kg of coffee per person annually — nearly four cups a day!

mixed.question: How many bones does an adult human body have?
mixed.fact: Babies are born with about 270 bones, but many fuse together as they grow!

mixed.question: Which famous scientist's brain was stolen during his autopsy in 1955?
mixed.fact: Pathologist Thomas Harvey removed Einstein's brain without permission and kept it for 40 years!

mixed.question: What is the smallest country in the world by area?
mixed.fact: Vatican City is only 0.44 square kilometers — smaller than most golf courses!

mixed.question: Which element has the chemical symbol 'W'?
options: Both A and B are correct
mixed.fact: The symbol 'W' comes from 'Wolfram,' the German name for tungsten, still used in many languages!

mixed.question: Who painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel?
mixed.fact: Michelangelo painted the ceiling mostly standing up, not lying down as commonly believed!

mixed.question: What animal's fingerprints are virtually indistinguishable from human fingerprints?
mixed.fact: Koala fingerprints are so similar to humans' that they've confused forensic investigators at crime scenes!

science.question: What is the hardest natural substance on Earth?
science.fact: Diamonds are so hard they can only be scratched by other diamonds!

science.question: Which planet in our solar system has the most moons?
science.fact: Saturn has over 140 confirmed moons, surpassing Jupiter's count in recent discoveries!

science.question: What gas do plants primarily absorb from the atmosphere during photosynthesis?
science.fact: Plants convert about 6 trillion kilograms of CO₂ into oxygen and sugar every year!

science.question: Which animal has the highest blood pressure of any living creature?
science.fact: A giraffe's blood pressure is roughly twice that of humans to pump blood up its long neck!

science.question: How many bones does an adult human body typically have?
science.fact: Babies are born with about 270 bones, but many fuse together as they grow!

science.question: What phenomenon causes the Earth's tides?
options: Gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun
science.fact: The Moon's gravity is the dominant force, but the Sun contributes about one-third of tidal effects!

science.question: Which element has the highest melting point of all known elements?
science.fact: Tungsten melts at 3,422°C — so hot it's used in rocket nozzles and light bulb filaments!

science.question: In what year was the structure of DNA first described by Watson and Crick?
science.fact: Rosalind Franklin's X-ray crystallography was crucial to the discovery but often went unrecognized!

science.question: What is the name of the supercontinent that existed about 335 million years ago?
science.fact: Pangaea means 'all lands' in Greek — every continent was once a single massive landmass!

science.question: Which organelle is responsible for producing ATP, the cell's main energy currency?
science.fact: Mitochondria have their own DNA, supporting the theory they were once independent bacteria!

history.question: In what year did the Berlin Wall fall?
history.fact: East German guards were never officially ordered to open the gates — it happened by confusion!

history.question: Who was the first President of the United States?
history.fact: George Washington was the only president unanimously elected by the Electoral College — twice!

history.question: Which ancient civilization built the Machu Picchu complex in Peru?
history.fact: Machu Picchu was unknown to the outside world until American historian Hiram Bingham publicized it in 1911.

history.question: Which empire was ruled by Suleiman the Magnificent?
history.fact: Suleiman personally led Ottoman armies in conquering Belgrade, Rhodes, and most of Hungary!

history.question: What was the name of the ship that brought the Pilgrims to America in 1620?
history.fact: The Mayflower was only about 100 feet long — roughly the size of a volleyball court!

history.question: In what year did the Chernobyl nuclear disaster occur?
history.fact: The Chernobyl exclusion zone has become an unexpected wildlife sanctuary with wolves, horses, and lynx.

history.question: Which medieval queen was married to both King Louis VII of France and King Henry II of England?
history.fact: Eleanor of Aquitaine was one of the wealthiest and most powerful women in medieval Europe.

history.question: How many days did the Cuban Missile Crisis last in October 1962?
history.fact: Soviet officer Vasili Arkhipov single-handedly prevented a nuclear torpedo launch during the crisis!

history.question: Which ancient library, considered the largest of the ancient world, was located in Egypt?
history.fact: The Library of Alexandria may have held up to 400,000 scrolls at its peak!

history.question: Who served as the youngest British Prime Minister, taking office at age 24 in 1783?
history.fact: Pitt the Younger led Britain through the French Revolutionary Wars and served as PM for nearly 19 years.

popculture.question: Which TV show features a character named Eleven who has telekinetic powers?
popculture.fact: Millie Bobby Brown shaved her head for the role and was only 12 during the first season!

popculture.question: What is the highest-grossing animated film of all time (as of early 2026)?
popculture.fact: Inside Out 2 surpassed Frozen II to claim the animated box office crown in 2024!

popculture.question: Who played Barbie in the 2023 blockbuster film directed by Greta Gerwig?
popculture.fact: The Barbie movie earned over $1.4 billion worldwide, making it the biggest film of 2023!

popculture.question: Which K-pop group released the album 'Map of the Soul: 7' and performed at multiple sold-out stadium tours?
popculture.fact: BTS was the first K-pop act to top the Billboard Hot 100 with a Korean-language song!

popculture.question: In what year did the Marvel Cinematic Universe debut with 'Iron Man'?
popculture.fact: Robert Downey Jr. was considered a risky casting choice but became the face of the MCU!

popculture.question: Which streaming platform originally released 'Squid Game'?
popculture.fact: Creator Hwang Dong-hyuk spent over a decade trying to get Squid Game produced before Netflix picked it up!

popculture.question: How many Grammy Awards did Beyoncé win in a single night at the 2023 ceremony, setting a new all-time record?
popculture.fact: That night brought Beyoncé to 32 total Grammys, making her the most decorated artist in Grammy history!

popculture.question: Which video game franchise features a battle royale mode called 'Warzone'?
popculture.fact: Warzone attracted over 100 million players within its first year of launch!

popculture.question: Which Oscar-winning film features the dialogue 'Everything, everywhere, all at once' and stars Michelle Yeoh?
popculture.fact: Michelle Yeoh became the first Asian woman to win the Academy Award for Best Actress!

popculture.question: Which pop star's 'Eras Tour' became the first concert tour in history to gross over $1 billion?
popculture.fact: The Eras Tour spanned over 140 shows and reportedly boosted local economies of every city it visited!

geography.question: What is the smallest country in the world by area?
geography.fact: Vatican City is only about 44 hectares — smaller than many golf courses!

geography.question: Which river is the longest in Africa?
geography.fact: The Nile stretches approximately 6,650 km, flowing through 11 different countries.

geography.question: On which continent would you find the Atacama Desert?
geography.fact: Parts of the Atacama went without any recorded rainfall for over 500 years!

geography.question: Which country has the most time zones, including overseas territories?
geography.fact: France spans 12 time zones thanks to its overseas territories scattered across the globe.

geography.question: What is the deepest lake in the world?
geography.fact: Lake Baikal reaches 1,642 meters deep and holds about 20% of the world's fresh surface water.

geography.question: Which African country is entirely surrounded by South Africa?
geography.fact: Lesotho is one of only three countries in the world completely enclosed by a single country.

geography.question: How many countries does the equator pass through?
geography.fact: The equator crosses 13 countries, including surprising ones like the Maldives and Kiribati.

geography.question: Which strait separates Europe from Africa at their closest point?
geography.fact: At its narrowest, the Strait of Gibraltar is only 14.3 km wide — visible to the naked eye!

geography.question: What is the only country that borders both the Caspian Sea and the Persian Gulf?
geography.fact: Iran's geography spans from Caspian wetlands in the north to Persian Gulf deserts in the south.

geography.question: Which island nation is located at the junction of the Indian Ocean and the Coral Sea?
geography.fact: Indonesia consists of over 17,000 islands, making it the world's largest archipelagic state.

sports.question: How many players are on a standard basketball team on the court at one time?
sports.fact: Five players per side has been the rule since basketball's earliest official games in the 1890s!

sports.question: Which country has won the most FIFA World Cup titles?
sports.fact: Brazil has won the World Cup five times — more than any other nation in history!

sports.question: In what sport would you perform a 'triple axel'?
sports.fact: The triple axel involves 3.5 rotations in the air — the only jump that launches going forward!

sports.question: Who holds the record for the most home runs in a single MLB season?
sports.fact: Barry Bonds smashed 73 home runs in 2001, a record that still stands decades later!

sports.question: Which NFL team completed an undefeated season in 1972, going 17-0?
sports.fact: The 1972 Miami Dolphins remain the only NFL team to finish a perfect season with a Super Bowl win!

sports.question: In tennis, what is the term for a score of 40-40?
sports.fact: The word 'deuce' likely comes from the French 'à deux,' meaning both players need two points to win!

sports.question: Which athlete has won the most Olympic gold medals in history?
sports.fact: Michael Phelps won an astonishing 23 Olympic gold medals across five Summer Games!

sports.question: What is the diameter of a regulation basketball hoop in inches?
sports.fact: At 18 inches wide, the rim is nearly twice the diameter of a basketball — yet shots still miss!

sports.question: Which boxer was known as 'The Greatest' and famously said 'Float like a butterfly, sting like a bee'?
sports.fact: Muhammad Ali's real name was Cassius Clay — he changed it after converting to Islam in 1964!

sports.question: In cricket, how many runs are scored when the ball is hit over the boundary without bouncing?
sports.fact: A 'six' is cricket's equivalent of a home run — clearing the boundary rope on the full!
